chapter_summary: Garuda, the immensely powerful bird who travels at will, returns across the great ocean to his mother Vinata, who has been reduced to slavery after losing a wager to her co-wife Kadru. Kadru orders Vinata to carry her to the beautiful dwelling of the Nagas in a corner of the ocean, and commands Garuda to carry her serpent-sons (the pannagas). As Garuda flies near the sun, the serpents are scorched by the sun's rays and faint. Seeing her sons in this stricken state, Kadru breaks into a long hymn of praise to Indra (Shakra), begging him to send rain to save the serpents. The hymn exalts Indra as the supreme protector and the all-encompassing cosmic power: the cloud, wind, lightning and thunderbolt; the creator and destroyer of the worlds; the sun and light of all beings; identified even with Vishnu and Soma; the embodiment of Time itself (muhurta, tithi, fortnights, seasons, year) and of the whole earth, sky and ocean; the one worshipped by sages and brahmanas through Vedic sacrifice and the Vedangas.

story.title: Kadru's Hymn to Indra and the Scorching of the Serpents
story.arc: Garuda returns to his enslaved mother Vinata across the ocean. Kadru, exploiting her victory in the wager, commands Vinata to bear her and Garuda to bear the serpent-sons to the Nagas' ocean dwelling. Mid-flight Garuda soars near the sun and the serpents faint from its rays. Stricken with fear for her sons, Kadru pours out a sweeping hymn to Indra, imploring him as the cosmic rain-bringer and supreme protector to flood the sky with water and rescue them.
story.timeline_note: Astika Parva; immediately after Vinata loses the wager and falls into slavery, and before Indra answers with rain. Garuda is here a youth carrying the serpents, prior to his quest for amrita.
story.comic_potential: The cosmic irony of a serpent-mother frantically praising the very god of rain to save the snakes that her rivalry set in peril, while the great bird Garuda obediently ferries his future enemies high enough to nearly cook them in the sun.

teachings.teaching: In distress, Kadru invokes Indra as the supreme refuge and the one able to create abundant water, calling him the cloud, wind, lightning-fire, the scatterer of clouds and the terrible thunderbolt, the very flood that can save the scorched serpents.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework the deity implored against the burning sun is the inner reservoir of soma-nectar that cools the seeker scorched by uncontrolled solar (pingala) heat; rain here is the descending grace that quenches the fire of the senses.

teachings.teaching: The hymn exalts the one deity as both creator and destroyer of the worlds, the unconquered light of all beings, the sun and the radiant one, the great wondrous being who is king, best of gods, even Vishnu and the final refuge.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the single power that is simultaneously creator, sustainer and dissolver, and the light within all beings, is the awakened consciousness at the sahasrara, before which the named gods are aspects of the one inner sun.

teachings.teaching: The deity is identified with Time itself and its every division: the nectar and Soma, the muhurta, tithi, lava and kshana, the bright and dark fortnights, the kala, kashtha and truti, the year, seasons, months, nights and days.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the god-as-Time as the breath that meters the practitioner's days; mastering the divisions of time through pranayama is to touch the timeless one who underlies muhurta, tithi and kshana alike.

teachings.teaching: The whole cosmos is declared to be the deity's body: the excellent earth with its mountains and forests, the sun-lit darkness-free sky, and the great ocean teeming with sea-monsters, waves, makaras and fishes.
teachings.lens_note: In the lens this all-form (vishvarupa-like) vision teaches the seeker to behold the single consciousness pervading earth, sky and sea, dissolving the sense of a separate self into the one cosmic body.

teachings.teaching: The deity is the goal of all worship and sacrifice: praised by joyful sages, drinking Soma at the rite and receiving oblations consecrated by vashatkara, sung of in the Vedangas, for whose sake the foremost brahmanas devoted to sacrifice study the Vedangas with all the Vedas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma treats outward yajna as a figure for inner offering: the true adhvara is the practitioner offering breath and ego into the inner fire, the Vedangas being limbs of the discipline that point the devotee toward the one they really worship.
